epigraph.text: There is one body and one Spirit—just as you were called to the one hope that belongs to your call.
epigraph.quote: The Riemann Mapping Theorem is one of the most remarkable and far-reaching theorems in all of analysis.

content.text:
The Riemann Mapping Theorem asserts that all simply connected proper 
subdomains of $\C$ are conformally equivalent. This profound result 
reveals the unity underlying apparent geometric diversity.

content.title: Riemann Mapping Theorem
content.content:
Let $D$ be a simply connected domain in $\C$ with $D \neq \C$. Then 
there exists a conformal bijection $f: D \to \mathbb{D}$, where 
$\mathbb{D} = \{z : |z| < 1\}$ is the unit disk.

Moreover, if $z_0 \in D$ is specified, there is a unique such $f$ 
satisfying $f(z_0) = 0$ and $f'(z_0) > 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The proof is beyond our scope but uses the theory of normal families 
and an extremal argument: among all conformal maps from $D$ to $\mathbb{D}$ 
with $f(z_0) = 0$, we seek the one maximizing $|f'(z_0)|$.

content.content:
The theorem fails for $D = \C$: by Liouville's theorem, every bounded 
entire function is constant, so there is no conformal map from $\C$ 
onto the bounded disk.

content.text:
The Riemann Mapping Theorem tells us that conformal geometry sees only 
topology. A disk, a square, an ellipse, a half-plane, a slit plane—all 
simply connected domains (except $\C$) are conformally the same.

content.problem:
Consider the following domains:

- The upper half-plane $\{z : \im(z) > 0\}$

- The first quadrant $\{z : \re(z) > 0, \im(z) > 0\}$

- The strip $\{z : 0 < \im(z) < 1\}$

- The region outside the unit disk $\{z : |z| > 1\}$... but wait, 
this is *not* simply connected!
The first three are conformally equivalent to the disk. The fourth is 
not simply connected (loops around infinity cannot shrink), so the 
theorem does not apply.

content.title: Carathéodory's Theorem
content.content:
If $D$ is a simply connected domain bounded by a Jordan curve (a simple 
closed curve), then the Riemann map $f: D \to \mathbb{D}$ extends 
continuously to a bijection from $\overline{D}$ onto $\overline{\mathbb{D}}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: This ensures that "nice" boundaries map to nice boundaries.

marginNotes.text:
There is one body and one Spirit—just 
as you were called to the one hope that belongs to your call—one Lord, 
one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all.

marginNotes.author: Bernhard Riemann (1851)
marginNotes.text:
The theorem was announced by 
Riemann in his doctoral dissertation, though the first complete proof 
came later.
